headline: Wheat Dumps 14¢, Corn Slips on Dollar Strength
subheadline: Crude flat, dollar up — export math just got tougher for spring.
teaser: Dollar surge pressures grains while Illinois preps for 2-inch rain event.

one_number.why: Heaviest Thursday (1.28 inches). Fields already at 67% humidity. Burndown and early prep work window closes Wednesday.

field_call.label: PLAN AHEAD
field_call.detail: Illinois gets soaked Thu-Fri. Iowa/Nebraska stay workable through Thursday. Get burndown done by Wednesday if you're south.

sections.title: Grains & Oilseeds
bullets: Corn $4.45¼, down 2¾¢. Dec '26 at $4.69¼. The 24¢ carry says the market wants you to store — but basis better reflect that.
Beans $11.61¾, down 8½¢. Meal at $312.50, oil at 62.68¢. Crush margins squeezed as meal slides faster than beans.
Wheat $5.76¼, down 14½¢ in a 2.5% dump. Dollar strength killed export hopes. Spring wheat plantings looking attractive at sub-$6 futures.

sections.title: Livestock & Dairy
bullets: Live cattle $233.65, up $1.42. Feeders $353.70, up $2.73. Cash trade likely $189-$192 this week as packers need supplies.
Hogs $95.58, basically flat. Cutout holding steady — pork demand baseline, not exciting.
Class III milk exploded $2.29 (up 15%) to $17.21. Cheese markets finally woke up. Lock in Q2 milk if you haven't.

sections.title: Weather & Fieldwork
bullets: Central Illinois gets 1.87 inches through Friday — 1.28 inches Thursday alone. Fields at 67% RH already. No spray window Thu-Fri.
Iowa and Nebraska stay mostly dry until Friday (0.34 and 0.16 inches total). Good burndown window Monday through Thursday.
Temps: 40s to low 70s by Friday across the Belt. Soil temps climbing but wet conditions will slow warmup in Illinois.
Bottom line: Get pre-plant done by Wednesday if you're Illinois/Indiana. Western Belt has a full week of decent conditions.

sections.title: Energy & Macro
bullets: Crude $71.03, down 28¢. Diesel holding steady but not dropping. Anhydrous prices jumped $60-$80/ton on Iran supply fears (see Policy).
Dollar at 98.51, up 66 points. That's a 0.67% move — enough to make our beans and corn less competitive vs Brazilian exports.
Natural gas $2.98, up 9¢. Doesn't help NH3 costs but at least it's not spiking harder.

sections.title: Policy & Trade
bullets: Iran tensions closed Strait of Hormuz — urea prices spiked $60-$80/ton as one-third of global fertilizer trade is threatened. Lock remaining N needs now.
USDA January crush: 228 million bushels soybeans (6.84M tons), 505M bushels corn to ethanol. Steady demand, nothing shocking.
Dicamba back for 2026-2027 seasons per EPA, but legal fights continue. Don't assume it sticks — have backup weed plans.

watch_list.label: Today
watch_list.item: Dollar direction — any pullback helps grain exports and could stabilize beans.

watch_list.label: This Week
watch_list.item: Thursday's Illinois rain event. If models shift heavier, expect basis to firm.

watch_list.label: Horizon
watch_list.item: March 31 Prospective Plantings report. Acreage expectations will set tone for spring rally or fade.

watch_list.label: Risk
watch_list.item: Dollar keeps climbing and Brazil undercuts us on beans. We're already down 8½¢ with export math weakening.

watch_list.label: Opportunity
watch_list.item: December corn at $4.69¼ with 24¢ carry rewards storage. Check your basis offers before you commit.

the_more_you_know.title: Milk's Biggest One-Day Pop
the_more_you_know.body: Class III milk jumping $2.29 in one day is a 15% move — the kind of volatility usually reserved for hogs or wheat. When cheese buyers panic, dairy producers finally get a break.

daily_quote.text: The farmer has to be an optimist or he wouldn't still be a farmer.
daily_quote.attribution: Will Rogers, humorist and ranch kid

source_summary: Data from CME settlement prices, NOAA/NWS weather, USDA NASS reports, and curated ag news sources including Farm Progress, Brownfield, and regional outlets.
